articles.label: Analysis
articles.headline: Escambia County's $47 Million Stormwater Problem Has No Fix in Sight
articles.dek: Three years after voters approved a dedicated fee, the county has spent more on consultants than culverts. Records show why.
brief: Escambia County has collected $47.2 million in stormwater fees since 2023 but completed only 12 of 89 planned drainage projects
Public records show $8.3 million went to engineering consultants while $6.1 million funded actual construction
County staff blame federal permitting delays and inflation, but internal emails reveal years of deferred maintenance decisions
articles.body:
The culvert under Fairfield Drive collapsed on a Tuesday morning last September. By noon, water had swallowed three cars and flooded the first floor of Myrtle Grove Baptist Church. By evening, Escambia County Commissioner Jeff Bergosh stood in rubber boots promising residents that help was coming.

"This is exactly why we passed the stormwater fee," Bergosh told the crowd. "We're going to fix this."

Seven months later, the culvert remains collapsed. A temporary steel plate covers the hole. Orange barrels narrow traffic to one lane. The church has gutted its fellowship hall and is still waiting on insurance.

Fairfield Drive is one of 89 drainage projects the county identified as critical when voters approved a dedicated stormwater fee in November 2022. The fee—$84 annually for a typical single-family home—was supposed to generate roughly $15 million per year exclusively for drainage infrastructure. Three years in, the county has collected $47.2 million.

It has completed 12 projects.

Where the Money Went

A Flightline analysis of county expenditure records obtained through public records requests shows a familiar pattern: more money flowing to consultants than to concrete.

Since the fee took effect in January 2023, Escambia County has spent $8.3 million on engineering and consulting services related to stormwater projects. It has spent $6.1 million on actual construction.

The largest recipient is Atkins North America, a Jacksonville-based engineering firm that holds the county's master stormwater planning contract. Atkins has received $3.7 million for project design, environmental assessments and "program management support." The firm declined to comment for this article.

CDM Smith, a Boston-headquartered consultant, received $2.1 million for watershed modeling studies. Jacobs Engineering collected $1.4 million for permitting assistance.

The remaining $32.8 million sits in a dedicated fund, accruing interest while projects stall.

The Permitting Bottleneck

County officials point to federal regulations as the primary obstacle. Any project that affects wetlands or waterways requires permits from the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers, a process that routinely takes 18 to 24 months.

"We can't just start digging," said Matt Posner, the county's stormwater management director, in a February interview. "Every one of these projects touches jurisdictional waters. We're at the mercy of the Corps."

The Army Corps of Engineers Jacksonville District confirmed that Escambia County has 23 permit applications pending, with an average wait time of 19 months. Spokesperson Linda Chen said the district is "working through a significant backlog" affecting projects across North Florida.

But internal county emails obtained by The Flightline suggest the permitting delays were predictable—and preventable.

In March 2021, nearly two years before voters approved the stormwater fee, the county's environmental compliance officer warned that the Corps had implemented new wetland delineation requirements that would extend permit timelines. The email recommended the county begin pre-application consultations for priority projects.

"If we wait until funding is secured, we'll lose 2-3 years on the front end," wrote compliance officer Rachel Torres.

The county did not begin pre-application consultations until August 2023—eight months after fee collection started.

Decades of Deferred Maintenance

The current backlog did not materialize overnight. Escambia County's drainage infrastructure has been deteriorating for decades, a product of growth that outpaced investment and budgets that prioritized more visible projects.

A 2019 county assessment identified $340 million in stormwater infrastructure needs. The report documented culverts installed in the 1960s that had never been inspected, drainage easements that had been built over by homeowners and outfall pipes that discharged directly into Pensacola Bay without any treatment.

That assessment sat largely unaddressed until 2022, when a series of flash floods during an unusually wet spring pushed stormwater to the top of the political agenda. The floods damaged more than 200 homes, mostly in lower-income neighborhoods north of Interstate 10.

Commissioner Bergosh and then-Commissioner Lumon May championed the stormwater fee as a dedicated funding source that couldn't be raided for other purposes. Voters approved it with 58% support.

"People were promised this money would fix the flooding," said Marcus Webb, a Brownsville resident whose home has flooded three times since 2020. "My street's not even on the list anymore."

Webb is correct. The county's priority project list has been revised twice since 2023. His street, North Q Street, was ranked 34th in the original assessment. In the current version, it does not appear at all.

Shifting Priorities

County staff say the list changes reflect updated engineering assessments and available funding. Some projects initially deemed critical turned out to be less urgent upon detailed study. Others proved more expensive than estimated.

The Fairfield Drive culvert, for example, was initially budgeted at $1.2 million. Current estimates exceed $4 million due to unexpected soil conditions and the need to relocate utility lines.

"We'd rather do fewer projects right than rush into construction and have failures," Posner said.

But the revised list also reflects political pressure. Several projects added since 2023 are located in Commissioner Steven Barry's district, which saw the highest opposition to the stormwater fee in the 2022 vote. Barry did not respond to requests for comment.

Commissioner May, who left office in 2024, said in a phone interview that he regrets not building stronger oversight into the fee's enabling ordinance.

"We trusted that the money would be spent the way we promised," May said. "That was naive. There should have been a citizen board with real authority."

Inflation and Inertia

Construction costs have risen 31% since 2022, according to the Associated General Contractors of America. Materials that cost $100 when voters approved the fee now cost $131. Labor shortages have pushed wages higher still.

The county's original 10-year project timeline assumed 3% annual inflation. Reality has been far harsher.

"We're essentially four years behind before we've really started," said University of West Florida economist Rick Harper, who has studied regional infrastructure funding. "They collected money during the worst possible window for construction costs."

Harper noted that the county could have locked in prices by issuing bonds against the anticipated fee revenue, funding construction immediately rather than accumulating cash. The commission considered this approach in early 2023 but rejected it over concerns about debt levels.

That decision looks costly in hindsight. The $32.8 million sitting in the stormwater fund has earned roughly $2.4 million in interest. But the same projects that would have cost $32 million in 2023 will now cost an estimated $42 million.

"They lost $7 million by being conservative," Harper said. "That's real projects that won't get built."

What Happens Now

The Board of County Commissioners is scheduled to review the stormwater program at its April 17 meeting. Staff recommendations include increasing the annual fee by $24 to offset inflation, extending the project timeline from 10 years to 15 and hiring two additional in-house engineers to reduce reliance on consultants.

Posner said the county has learned from its early missteps and expects to complete 15 projects in the coming fiscal year—more than the previous three years combined.

"The consultants did necessary work," Posner said. "You can't build what you haven't designed. We're past that phase now."

Residents like Marcus Webb are skeptical.

"They told us three years ago the money would fix the flooding," Webb said. "Now they want more money and more time. At some point you have to actually dig a ditch."

The congregation at Myrtle Grove Baptist Church held Easter services last week in their gymnasium. The fellowship hall remains gutted. Pastor Jerome Williams said he calls the county every month for updates on Fairfield Drive.

"They're very polite," Williams said. "They tell me it's in the queue. Everything's always in the queue."

Outside the church, the orange barrels remain. The steel plate rattles when trucks pass over. And when the next heavy rain comes—as it always does along the Gulf Coast—the water will find the same low points it has found for decades.

The only difference now is that someone's being paid to study it.

articles.label: Feature
articles.headline: The Last Fish House on Pensacola Beach
articles.dek: Joe Patti's Seafood built an empire on the mainland. One family-owned competitor remains on the island, caught between rising rents and a changing shoreline.
brief: Innerarity Seafood Market is the last independent fish house operating on Pensacola Beach, holding out against rising commercial rents and corporate consolidation
Owner Danny Innerarity says his lease renewal this summer could triple his monthly costs from $4,200 to over $12,000
The family has sold fresh Gulf catch from the same stretch of Via de Luna since 1987, outlasting at least six other independent seafood operations
articles.body:
The cooler hums at 34 degrees. Danny Innerarity lifts a 40-pound red snapper by the gills and drops it on the stainless steel counter. The fish hit the beach three hours ago, pulled from 180 feet of water 60 miles south of here. By noon it will be filleted, scaled, and wrapped in white butcher paper for someone's Sunday dinner.

"That's the whole game," Innerarity says. "Boat to counter. No warehouse. No truck from Texas."

Innerarity Seafood Market sits in a concrete block building on Via de Luna Drive, across from the public parking lot at Casino Beach. It is the last independent fish house on Pensacola Beach. The hand-painted sign out front is sun-faded to the color of old bone. The building itself is older than most of the condominiums that now surround it.

Danny Innerarity is 58. His father opened this market in 1987, the year after Hurricane Elena scraped the island clean. His grandfather ran boats out of Bayou Chico before that. Three generations of Gulf fishing, and Danny figures he might be the last.

"The water's still here," he says. "The fish are still here. It's the land that's gotten expensive."

The Lease

Innerarity's current lease expires August 31. He pays $4,200 a month for 1,800 square feet of retail space and a loading dock. The property owner, a Destin-based LLC called Emerald Coast Holdings, has offered a renewal. The new rate: $12,600 monthly, a 200 percent increase.

"They sent the letter in January," Innerarity says. "I thought it was a typo."

It was not a typo. Commercial rents on Pensacola Beach have climbed steadily since 2020, accelerating after the Santa Rosa Island Authority loosened height restrictions on new development in 2023. A strip of beachfront that once held bait shops and beach bars now hosts luxury condo towers and boutique hotels.

Escambia County property records show the parcel containing Innerarity Seafood last sold in 2019 for $1.2 million. Zillow's commercial estimate now values it at $3.4 million.

"They can get more money turning this into a T-shirt shop," Innerarity says. "Or tearing it down and building condos. I understand that. I just don't know what to do about it."

The Competition That Wasn't

For decades, Pensacola Beach supported a handful of independent seafood markets. Anglers could sell their catch locally. Tourists could buy grouper that had been swimming that morning. The economics worked because commercial rents stayed manageable and the island's development remained modest.

That changed. One by one, the fish houses closed or moved to the mainland.

Gulf Breeze Seafood shut down in 2014. Captain Pete's relocated to Navy Boulevard in 2018. The Fish House on Pensacola Beach—not to be confused with the restaurant on Bayfront Parkway—closed during the pandemic and never reopened. Its building is now a real estate office.

Joe Patti's Seafood, the region's dominant player, operates from a sprawling facility on West Main Street in downtown Pensacola. The company has distribution contracts with restaurants across the Gulf Coast and ships product nationwide. Joe Patti's is a genuine local success story, a family business that scaled up and thrived.

But Joe Patti's doesn't have a location on Pensacola Beach. It has never tried to open one.

"The economics don't work for us there," said Frank Patti, the company's vice president of operations, in a phone interview. "Island rent, island logistics, island parking. We looked at it years ago and walked away."

That leaves Innerarity Seafood alone.

What Gets Lost

On a Thursday morning in late March, the market is busy. A retired couple from Montgomery selects amberjack steaks. A charter boat captain drops off a cooler of mahi-mahi for consignment sale. A line forms at the register.

Innerarity works the counter alongside Maria Gonzalez, who has filleted fish here for 22 years. She can break down a cobia in four minutes flat. The knife moves in quick, certain strokes.

"Danny's father taught me," Gonzalez says. "When I started I didn't know nothing about fish. Now I know everything."

The market sells what local boats catch: red snapper and grouper when the seasons are open, triggerfish and amberjack in between, whatever comes up on a given day. Innerarity posts the daily selection on a whiteboard by the door. No printed menus. The inventory changes too fast.

This model—boat to counter, no middlemen, no warehouse—is increasingly rare on the Gulf Coast. Most retail seafood now moves through distribution networks. Fish caught in Destin might travel to a processing facility in Louisiana before returning to a Pensacola grocery store.

"You gain efficiency," says Marcus Hill, a fisheries economist at the University of South Alabama. "You lose traceability. You lose freshness. You lose the direct relationship between the fishing fleet and the consumer."

Hill's research tracks the decline of independent fish markets across the northern Gulf. In 1990, his data shows 34 retail fish houses operating between Mobile Bay and Panama City. Today there are nine.

"These businesses served an ecological function," Hill says. "They kept small-boat fishing economically viable. When they disappear, the boats often follow."

The Math

Danny Innerarity's business is profitable. He would not share specific revenue figures, but he confirmed the market operates in the black and supports three full-time employees besides himself.

At $4,200 monthly rent, the numbers work. At $12,600, they do not.

"I'd have to raise prices 30, 40 percent," he says. "At that point I'm not competing with the grocery store anymore. I'm competing with restaurants. And I can't cook it for you."

He has considered alternatives. Moving to the mainland, like Captain Pete's did. Downsizing to a farmers market booth. Selling directly to restaurants instead of retail customers.

None of these options preserve what Innerarity Seafood currently is: a fish market on the beach, where tourists and locals can buy fresh Gulf catch from people who know where it came from.

"My father built this because he thought the beach should have a fish house," Innerarity says. "He thought that was obvious. Maybe it's not obvious anymore."

What Comes Next

Innerarity has until June 15 to accept or reject the new lease terms. He has not yet decided.

The property owner, Emerald Coast Holdings, did not respond to interview requests. Public records show the LLC is managed by a commercial real estate firm in Destin. The firm's website advertises "premium Gulf-front development opportunities."

If Innerarity Seafood closes or relocates, no obvious replacement exists. The building's zoning would permit another seafood market, but the economics that drove out every other independent operator would apply to any newcomer.

"Someone might try," Innerarity says. "I hope someone does. But they'll be starting from scratch, and they'll be paying the new rent from day one."

He turns back to the counter. A customer wants to know if the snapper is fresh. Innerarity points to the whiteboard: SNAPPER, MARCH 27, BOAT: MISS LINDA, CAPTAIN: TONY RAMOS.

"Tony caught that fish 60 miles out Tuesday morning," he says. "It was on ice by 6 a.m. You can call him yourself if you want."

The customer does not want to call. The customer wants three pounds, filleted.

Maria Gonzalez reaches for her knife.

articles.label: Military
articles.headline: NAS Pensacola Prepares for Largest Training Class Expansion Since 2019
articles.dek: The Blue Angels aren't the only thing growing at the base. A 15% increase in aviation students will test Pensacola's housing market this fall.
brief: Naval Air Station Pensacola will add approximately 450 additional student aviators and naval flight officers beginning in October, the largest single-year increase since 2019.
Base housing is already at 98% capacity, pushing an estimated 300 students and their families into the local rental market.
City and county officials are meeting with Navy leadership this week to discuss infrastructure strain and potential economic benefits.
articles.body:
The parking lot outside Saufley Field's primary training hangar was full by 6:45 a.m. last Tuesday. Flight instructors in olive flight suits walked between rows of T-6B Texan II turboprops, clipboards in hand. By 7:30, the first engine turned over.

This is the current pace of naval aviation training in Pensacola. By October, it's going to get faster.

Naval Air Station Pensacola will receive approximately 450 additional student aviators and naval flight officers in the 2027 fiscal year, according to a Navy statement released Friday. The 15% increase represents the largest single-year expansion of the "Cradle of Naval Aviation" training pipeline since 2019, when the service began scaling up pilot production to meet fleet demand.

"We're not just adding bodies," said Capt. Marissa Chen, commanding officer of Naval Air Training Command Pensacola, in a phone interview. "We're adding aircraft hours, instructor billets, and classroom capacity. This has been in planning for two years."

The housing problem

The expansion arrives in a housing market already stretched thin.

Base housing at NAS Pensacola operates at 98% capacity, according to figures provided by the installation's public affairs office. Corry Station, which handles information warfare training, sits at 94%. Neither facility can absorb the incoming students.

Navy officials estimate roughly 300 of the new arrivals will be married or have dependents. Those families will compete for rentals in a civilian market where vacancy rates have hovered below 4% since 2023.

"We're already hearing from landlords who've figured this out," said Escambia County Commissioner Jeff Bergosh, whose district includes much of the county's military-adjacent housing. "Some are holding units off the market until fall, anticipating they can get higher rents. That's not illegal. But it does make things harder for everyone else."

The average rent for a two-bedroom apartment in Pensacola rose to $1,485 in February, according to Zillow data. That's a 23% increase from February 2023.

Basic Allowance for Housing for an O-1 with dependents—the typical pay grade for a student aviator—currently sits at $1,647 per month for the Pensacola area. The margin is narrow.

Infrastructure questions

Housing isn't the only concern. Pensacola City Council member Teneka Leath said she's requested a meeting with base leadership to discuss traffic impacts, particularly along Navy Boulevard and Blue Angel Parkway.

"We've got school buses, commuters and military traffic all hitting the same roads at the same hours," Leath said. "Before we celebrate the economic boost, I want to know the Navy's plan for the squeeze."

That meeting is scheduled for Thursday at Pensacola City Hall. Escambia County Administrator Wes Moreno confirmed county staff will also attend.

The Navy's statement emphasized infrastructure investments already underway. A $34 million training facility at Saufley Field broke ground last September and is expected to open in early 2027. The base has also requested funding for a new simulator building that would reduce flight hour requirements for certain training phases.

Economic upside

Not everyone sees the expansion as a problem to manage.

FloridaWest Economic Development Council estimates each military student contributes roughly $47,000 annually to the local economy through housing, food, entertainment and vehicle expenses. The incoming class could represent $21 million in new spending.

"This is exactly the kind of growth Pensacola needs," said Scott Luth, FloridaWest's CEO. "These are young professionals with steady income. Many of them fall in love with the area and come back after their service. That's our future workforce."

Local businesses near the base have already begun preparing. Pensacola Aviation Services, which provides civilian flight training and aircraft maintenance, said it plans to hire four additional mechanics by September.

"When the Navy's busy, we're busy," said owner Dale McCutcheon. "Their overflow becomes our opportunity."

The 2019 shadow

The last major expansion, in 2019, was followed within months by the December 6 shooting at NAS Pensacola that killed three sailors and wounded eight others. A Saudi Arabian aviation student carried out the attack before being killed by sheriff's deputies.

In the years since, the Navy has overhauled its international military student vetting procedures. Security protocols at the main gate have been tightened. But the memory lingers.

"I think about it," Chen acknowledged. "Every commanding officer here thinks about it. We've made changes. Significant changes. But you never assume you're done."

The 2027 class will include students from allied nations, though the Navy declined to specify which countries or how many. International students currently make up roughly 5% of the training population at NAS Pensacola.

What happens next

The first wave of additional students is expected to arrive in October. Full capacity should be reached by January 2027.

Pensacola Mayor D.C. Reeves said the city welcomes the growth but urged realistic expectations.

"We're proud to be a Navy town," Reeves said in a written statement. "We're also a city of 55,000 people with aging infrastructure and a housing shortage. We can be both grateful and honest about the challenges."

Thursday's meeting at City Hall is open to the public. It begins at 2 p.m. in the second-floor conference room.
